title: Dynamical pseudopotentials
authors: Matteo Quinzi, Tommaso Chiarotti, Nicola Marzari
abstract: Pseudopotential theory has greatly driven first-principles calculations in materials, replacing the explicit treatment of the chemically inert core electrons with an effective potential acting only on the valence states. This is inherently an embedding problem, where tracing out the core electrons can be formulated in terms of a dynamical embedding potential. Motivated by this perspective, we first introduce a framework for dynamical (i.e., energy-dependent) pseudopotentials, showing how this leads to generalized norm-conservation conditions. Then, adopting a sum-over-poles representation, we disentangle the number of reference energies from the number of projectors; this allows to reproduce all-electron scattering at many reference energies with great accuracy and over very extended energy ranges. We further show that these pseudopotentials enter naturally into many-body total energy functionals, leading for the first time to a consistent and unified treatment of the all-electron atom, the pseudo-atom, and the solid within the same electronic-structure theory.
summary: This paper introduces a new class of energy-dependent pseudopotentials that improve the transferability of first-principles calculations to high-energy and excited states. By using a sum-over-poles approach, it achieves high accuracy without the numerical instabilities typical of multi-projector methods. This provides a unified and consistent treatment of all-electron and pseudo-atom properties within many-body theory.
main_problem: Conventional static pseudopotentials struggle with transferability in excited-state calculations and high-energy states, often suffering from numerical instabilities when increasing the number of projectors.
main_result: The authors develop a framework for dynamical (energy-dependent) pseudopotentials using a sum-over-poles representation that decouples the number of reference energies from the number of projectors, ensuring accurate all-electron scattering over wide energy ranges.
method: The method uses an embedding-problem formulation, a sum-over-poles (SOP) representation, and an algorithmic-inversion method to solve the nonlinear eigenvalue problem, combined with a variational strategy to optimize the basis set.
model_or_system: The framework treats the electronic structure of atoms and solids as an embedding problem where valence electrons are coupled to an auxiliary bath representing core degrees of freedom via an energy-dependent potential.
key_observables: Logarithmic derivative of the potential, dynamical augmentation charges, radial momentum distribution, and spectral density.
important_parameters: Core radius (Rc), reference energies, number of poles, and number of projectors.
main_assumptions: The derivation assumes spherical symmetry for atomic calculations and assumes the availability of radial all-electron and pseudo-orbitals.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the logarithmic derivative vs. energy for Cu and Er, demonstrating the match between AE and dynPP; supplementary figures show radial momentum distributions and wavefunction profiles.
paper_structure: Introduction of the dynamical embedding perspective, derivation of generalized norm-conservation, introduction of the sum-over-poles representation and algorithmic inversion, demonstration of numerical stability and efficiency, and validation via momentum-space analysis.
why_interesting: The use of an embedding-problem formulation and the treatment of energy-dependent potentials are highly relevant to many-body dynamics and the development of frequency-dependent self-energies in correlated systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Logarithmic derivative as a function of energy for the all-electron (AE) potential, and dynamical pseudopotential (dynPP) for the (left) copper d electrons at distance R = 2.1 (a.u.) and (right) erbium f electrons at distance R = 1.6 (a.u.). The reference energies are marked with black dots. In both cases the pseudopotential is built including up to seven reference energies, while only three projectors are used following the truncation strategy.
